segments.s309-g121: بدان is an imperative built from the prefix بـ + دان (literally 'know' or 'be aware'): here it introduces what the listener should note. تا introduces the following subordinate/purposive/conditional clause ('so that' / 'that'), and اگر begins the conditional 'if'. سازد is the classical present/subjunctive form of ساختن ('to make/prepare, here 'to launch/undertake'), which governs the action that Afrasiab (افرسیاب, a proper name) might carry out.
segments.s309-g122: بر او = 'against him' (بر marking the target with the clitic pronoun او). The second بر combines with شبیخون ('night-raid, surprise attack') to indicate what is being made ('to make a night-raid upon him'). به هنگام خواب is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of sleep' (به هنگام = 'at the time of'; خواب = 'sleep'). Word order here places the target and manner/time phrases after the verb سازد in the previous segment.
segments.s309-g123: گر is the conditional 'if'. آید سپاه literally 'an army comes' (verb + noun inversion is permitted in classical style). اندر آید ز پس expands the action: اندر آید = 'enters/comes in' and زِ پس = 'from the rear/behind' (ز = 'from', پس = 'after/behind'), so the clause describes forces arriving from behind.
segments.s309-g124: بماند is an impersonal/predicative form 'remains' / 'will be left'. نباشدش is the negative present/optative 'there is not for him' with the enclitic -ش ('for him'), so together they yield 'he will be left with no ...'. فریادرس ('rescuer, helper') is the noun that completes the sense: 'no rescuer remains for him.'
segments.s309-g73: فزون کرد is a two-word verbal phrase: فزون (comparative/increasing) + کرد (past of کردن 'to make/do') => 'made more' or 'professed more'. از این functions as a comparative 'than this'. با سیاوش وفا is an object phrase 'loyalty toward Siavash' (با = 'with/to', سیاوش = proper name, وفا = 'fidelity'). The whole segment therefore means 'he showed/made greater fidelity to Siavash than this.'
segments.s309-g74: زبان پر فسون بود is a nominal clause with بود as the copula in past ('was'): 'his tongue/speech was full of enchantment' (زبان here = 'speech/utterance', پر = 'full (of)', فسون = 'enchantment'). دل پرجفا is a parallel nominal phrase 'heart full of treachery/cruelty' (پرجفا is an adjectival compound meaning 'full of jفا/treachery'). The two halves are linked by asyndeton to contrast external speech and inner heart.
segments.s309-g75: سپهبد is a common noun here ('spahbed' = high military commander). به کژی نگیرد is a negative present/formal statement: نگیرد (from گرفتن) 'does not take/accept' with به کژی 'by crookedness' as the manner/means; فروغ is the object 'glory/radiance'. Word order is verb-final in the verbal phrase 'does not gain glory by crookedness.'
segments.s309-g76: This is a paired descriptive phrase. زبان خیره پر تاب: زبان ('speech') modified by خیره ('stunning/astonishing') with پر تاب 'full of radiance/brightness' — an adjectival cluster describing his speech. و دل پر دروغ is a coordinated phrase 'and a heart full of lies.' The adjectives follow the nouns (typical in classical Persian) and پر here is the preposed 'full (of)' expressed as a prefix to the following adjective.
segments.s309-g77: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون here means 'appears/comes into view' (classical usage). که introduces a relative clause describing that person: رایش 'his counsel/opinion' and نبردست 'is warlike/fit for battle' (an adjectival/epithet). و بس 'and sufficient/enough' completes the description. Overall: 'If one appears whose counsel is warlike and sufficient...'.
segments.s309-g78: جز از من نبرد ورا هست کس is an inverted existential clause: جز از من 'except for me' + نبرد (here 'to fight/battle' used as a verbal noun or object) + ورا 'him' + هست کس 'there is no person' (هست used as 'exists'). Literally: 'Except for me there exists no one who will fight him.'
segments.s309-g79: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet. به جایست is a fixed phrase 'is in his place/stands in position' (به + جای + است fused to جایست). و connects the clause to 'گیو دلیر' — 'Giv is brave.' The clause-order is nominal followed by predicate, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s309-g80: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause: پیکار جویند is a present plural verb phrase 'they seek (جویند) battle (پیکار)'. با پیل و شیر is the comitative phrase 'with elephant and lion(s)' (classical imagery for fighting mighty foes). The clause therefore says these men seek to do battle with elephants and lions.
segments.s309-g81: If-clause introduced by اگر sets up a conditional. شاه (subject) + با شاه (prepositional phrase “with a king”) precede the present/poetic verb جوید (3rd person singular, “seeks/wishes”), and نبرد is the object ‘battle’ placed after the verb — a common poetic order: “If a king with a king seeks battle.”
segments.s309-g82: چرا is a rhetorical “why.” باید is a modal auxiliary meaning “must/ought” or “should.” این دشت is the noun phrase “this plain,” and the predicate is the causative construction پر ... کرد: پر (here “full (of)”) + مرد (men) + کردن (کرد, “to make/cause”) → “to make this plain full of men.” Word order places the causative verb کرد at the end.
segments.s309-g83: نباشد is an archaic/poetic negative of باشد (here functioning like “there will not be” or “let there not be”). The following مرا (split in the token list as م + را) marks the experiencer/object (“me”) with را as the object marker. با تو “with you.” ز این بیش is a poetic comparative/adverbial phrase “beyond this / more than this.” جنگ is the noun “war” governed by the negated verblike نباشد: “there will be no more war between me and you than this.”
segments.s309-g84: ببینی is a jussive/subjunctive/poetic present of دیدن used here as an admonitory/future-facing ‘you will see’ or ‘see that…’. کنون “now” modifies the time, and روز تاریک و تنگ is a nominal phrase with two adjectives (تاریک “dark,” تنگ “narrow/tight”) describing the day: “you shall now see a dark and narrow day.” Word order places the verb before the time phrase, typical in poetry.
segments.s309-g85: Two coordinated verbs describe the messenger’s motion: فرستاده (messenger) + برگشت (returned) و آمد (and came). چو باد is a simile ‘like the wind’ modifying آمد (he came swiftly like the wind). The sequence emphasizes speed and suddenness.
segments.s309-g86: شنیده is a perfect/participial form ‘having heard / heard’; سراسر (‘throughout/all’) scopes over what was heard. بر او marks the person affected (“to him/against him”), and کرد یاد is a light-verb construction where کرد ‘made/did’ combines with the noun یاد (‘remembrance/news’) to mean ‘caused remembrance’ → ‘reminded him / told him (the news).’ Word order puts the participial phrase before the result clause.
segments.s309-g87: Predicate inversion: پر از درد شد is the verbal predicate ‘became full of pain/sorrow’ and جان افراسیاب is the subject placed after it — ‘Afrasiab’s soul became full of sorrow.’ شد is the past/preterite of شدن (to become).
segments.s309-g88: نکرد is the negative past ‘did not do.’ ایچ (ایچ/هیچ) means ‘at all/nothing’ emphasizing the negation. بر (here) marks direction/toward, so بر جنگ جستن is a verbal-nominal phrase ‘to seek/go to war’ (جستن as the infinitival/nominal verb) with شتاب ‘haste’ naming the action; together the phrase means he did not at all hasten to go to war. The clause is a compact negated nominalization: ‘he made no haste toward seeking war.’
segments.s309-g49: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'/'who'). این ترک بدساز مردم‌فریب is a noun phrase: این 'this' + ترک 'Turk' + two adjectival modifiers (بدساز 'bad-maker/troublemaker' and مردم‌فریب 'people‑deceiving'). The modifiers follow the noun in the line but conceptually qualify it as 'this treacherous, people‑deceiving Turk.'
segments.s309-g50: نبیند is a negative present/past narrative form of 'to see' ('he does not / did not see' depending on narrative tense); here with همی it has a habitual/continuous flavour ('never/does not at all see'). از بلندی نشیب is a prepositional phrase 'from (a) height, a descent' with از 'from' governing the noun phrase بلندی نشیب ('a descent from a high place').
segments.s309-g51: به چاره چنین is 'toward/for such a remedy/plan' (به + چاره + چنین). از کف ما is a possessive/agent phrase 'from our hand/possession' (از + کف + ما). بجست is a perfective/past form meaning 'he sought' (also can mean 'he leapt' in other contexts); here it functions as the main verb of the clause ('he sought/took up').
segments.s309-g52: نماید introduces the purpose/result or reported action ('he shows/does so' or 'he intends'), and که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. بر تخت ایران نشست is a verb phrase with بر تخت 'upon the throne' and ایران as the place/possessive, نشست is the verb 'sat/settled' (here 'take his seat' on the throne). The whole clause expresses the aim/result: that he sit on the Iranian throne.
segments.s309-g53: ز here is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from' or 'according to.' آورد is used in the sense 'relates/reports' (brings a report), and چندین modifies it ('many'/'several accounts'); بگوید همی is a habitual/iterative phrase 'he would say continually' (بگوید as reported-speech verb with همی marking repeated/continued action). The word order places the source ('from many reports') before the reporting verb.
segments.s309-g54: مگر introduces an exception or conditional 'unless.' دخمه 'tomb/vault' is the direct object; شیده is an adjectival modifier meaning 'love‑struck' or 'mad with love' (a poetic epithet); جوید is the present/stative verb 'seeks/searches' and همی again gives habitual/continuous sense. Together: 'unless he is continually seeking the tomb of the love‑struck.'
segments.s309-g55: نبیره فریدن و پور پشنگ is a coordinated noun phrase naming noble descendants: نبیره 'scion/offspring' + فریدن (here a proper name, Fereydun) and پور 'son/descendant' + پشنگ (Pashang). The two noun phrases are linked by و 'and' to list the prestigious lineages.
segments.s309-g56: به آورد با او is a verbal phrase 'to bring with him' (به + آورد 'to bring' + با او 'with him'). مرا را نیست ننگ is an idiomatic negated predicate meaning 'for me there is no shame' (مرا/م + را marks the experiencer/object 'to me', نیست 'is not', ننگ 'shame'); word order places the experiencer before the negated copula as is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s309-g89: This clause follows an older Persian word order where the object 'سپه را' (the host, with را marking a definite object) precedes the verb. 'به جنگ اندر آورد' is a compound verbal idea: 'به ... اندر آورد' = 'brought into (to) battle.' 'آورد' is the past-tense verb 'brought', and the subject 'شاه' appears after the verb (a common poetic/orderly placement) = 'the king (did this)'.
segments.s309-g90: 'بجنبید' here reports the movement (past/perfect sense 'moved' or 'stirred') of the army; it is followed by 'ناچار' meaning 'compelled/forced', and then the noun phrase 'دیگر سپاه' ('the other army'). The word order is somewhat inverted poetically: literally 'moved — compelled — the other host', i.e. 'the other host was forced to move.'
segments.s309-g91: The repeated 'یکی ... و یکی ...' is a distributive pattern meaning 'one (group) with ... and another with ...' — i.e. 'some slowly and some quickly.' 'با درنگ' = 'with delay/hesitation' and 'با شتاب' = 'with haste.' The subject (fighters/people) is omitted but implied.
segments.s309-g92: 'زمین شد' uses 'شد' (became) to predicate a change of state: 'the earth became ...'. 'به کردار' is a fixed, somewhat idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/likeness of' (here translated 'like'), and 'دریای آب' literally 'sea of water' is a poetic simile. Word order places the verb before the simile phrase, typical of Persian.
segments.s309-g93: The opening 'ز' marks cause or source ('from'). 'باریدن تیر' is a metaphorical noun phrase 'the falling/raining of arrows.' 'گفتی' here functions parenthetically (literally 'one might say' or 'it was as if said') — a poetic device introducing the simile — and 'ز ابر' specifies 'from the cloud(s)'. The sequence paints the image 'as if arrows rained from the clouds.'
segments.s309-g94: 'همی' with a past tense verb ('بارید') gives a continuous or iterative sense ('kept raining' / 'continued to fall'). 'ژاله' here is used metaphorically for small drops ('dew' or droplets), and 'بارید بر خود و ببر' means 'fell upon himself and the tiger' (i.e., both the rider/warrior and his tiger—bبر—were showered).
segments.s309-g95: The preposition 'ز' ('from') introduces the starting time 'شبگیر' (a poetic time-word related to night/nightfall), and 'تا' marks the endpoint 'until'. 'گشت' is the past of 'گشتن' meaning 'became/turned' and applies to 'خورشید لعل' — 'the sun (turned) ruby/deep red' — a vivid image of sunrise or blood-red light.
segments.s309-g96: 'زمین پر ز خون بود' literally 'the earth was full of blood' ('پر' = full (of), 'ز' = of), with 'بود' as the past copula ('was'). The locative phrase 'در زیر نعل' ('under/in beneath the horseshoe') specifies where the blood lay — under the horses' hoofs — with the usual poetic inversion of elements.
segments.s309-g65: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause here meaning "if/when." پیمان یزدان کنی combines noun + divine name + the 2nd‑singular present/subjunctive form کنی (from کردن) to mean "you make/pledge a covenant to God." با نیا means "with the ancestors/forefathers;" word order is flexible and poetic, so the preposition با follows the verb phrase.
segments.s309-g66: نشاید is impersonal and negative: "it is not fitting/it ought not to be." که introduces the content clause. در دل بود کیمیا = "the elixir was/should be in the heart;" بود is the simple copula (here rendering existence/state), دل = "heart," and کیمیا = "elixir/secret." The whole clause is the thing judged unfitting by نشاید.
segments.s309-g67: This is a poetic imperative: به انبوه لشکر به جنگ اندر آر = "bring/lead the numerous army into the fight." The first به marks direction/beneficiary toward the host ("to/for the host"), انبوه لشکر = "the numerous army," the second به + جنگ = "into battle," اندر آر is a compressed poetic verb phrase meaning "put/bring into" (an archaic/poetic use — here functioning as the command).
segments.s309-g68: سخن چند آلوده نابکار: سخن = "words/speech." چند here means "some/a few." آلوده is used morally here ("tainted/impure") and نابکار is an adjective "wicked/malefactor," qualifying those words. The phrase comments negatively on certain utterances.
segments.s309-g69: ز + name here functions «on/at the news/from (Rostam).» چو بشنید introduces a temporal clause "when he heard." بشنید is past 3rd‑person "he heard." In poetic word order the subject خسرو follows the verb, and سخن is the object: "When Khosrow heard the speech (from) Rostam..."
segments.s309-g70: یکی دیگر اندیشه افگند بن = literally "he cast/laid one other thought (the) root." یکی دیگر modifies اندیشه as "one other/another thought." افگند is past "he threw/cast/put" and بن ("root/foundation") is an object/complement showing he planted or set that thought at its root — idiomatically, "he conceived/formed another thought."
segments.s309-g71: به گوینده گفت = "(he) said to the speaker." به marks the indirect object/recipient. گفت is the past verb "said" (poetic register); the following noun phrase این بداندیش مرد is the content of what was said (direct object or quoted phrase): "this ill‑intentioned man."
segments.s309-g72: چنین با من آویخت اندر نبرد = a reported clause: چنین "thus/so," با من "to/against me" (with + pronoun marks the person affected), آویخت is past "he clung to/fastened on," and اندر نبرد "in the battle." Together: "he thus clung to me in the fight."
segments.s309-g1: وز here is an archaic fused particle (و + ز[=از]) introducing the phrase; آن پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that'; برآمد is past tense, 3rd-person singular 'arose/issued forth'; ز لشکر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the army/host' (ز is the classical form of از); خروش is the noun 'uproar/cry' and functions as the semantic subject that 'arose' (poetic word order places the verb before the source and the noun).
segments.s309-g2: زمین و زمان is an idiomatic paired subject 'earth and time' meaning 'the world'; شد is the past of شدن 'became' used as a linking verb; پر از is the idiom 'full of' (شد پر از X = 'became full of X'); جنگ و جوش are coordinated nouns 'war and tumult', with جوش used metaphorically as 'tumult/boiling up'.
segments.s309-g3: ستاره پدید آمد is a verbal clause 'a star appeared/was revealed' (پدید آمد = 'appeared'); از introduces the source 'from'; تیره is an adjective 'dark/ominous' modifying the following noun; گرد here is a poetic noun 'dust/whirl (of dust)' used as the material/source ('from a dark dust-whirl'). The phrase places the source after the verb in poetic inversion.
segments.s309-g4: رخ زرد خورشید is a nominal phrase 'the yellow face of the sun' (رخ = 'face/appearance', زرد = adjective 'yellow', خورشید in genitive relation 'of the sun'); شد is past of شدن 'became'; لاژورد is a color/material name 'lapis-lazuli/azure' used predicatively ('became lapis-lazuli'). Word order (noun + adjectival modifier + possessor) reflects classical/poetic Persian compression.
segments.s309-g5: سپهدار ترکان is a genitival construction 'the commander of the Turks' (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezafe); از آن انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from that assembly/council' that specifies source or context for the action in the next clause.
segments.s309-g6: گزین کرد is the verbal phrase 'chose' (گزین-کردن = 'to select' with کرد as the past auxiliary); کار آزموده is a compound adjectival phrase 'experienced/proven in work/deed' modifying the following numeral phrase; دو تن is a classical counting phrase 'two persons' (تن is a classifier for people). The object 'کار آزموده دو تن' follows the verb in this poetic clause.
segments.s309-g7: پیامی فرستاد is 'he sent a message' (پیامی = 'a message', فرستاد past 'sent'); نزدیک شاه is a locative/benefactive phrase 'near the king' meaning 'to the king's presence' or 'to the king' as recipient. The direct object comes before the verb, which is common in Persian prose/poetry.
segments.s309-g8: که introduces a reported clause 'that ...'; کردی here is used as the ethnico-geographic noun 'Kurds' (an older/poetic form), so the clause reports that 'many Kurds' were present; فراوان is 'many/abundant' modifying کردی; پس پشت راه is a poetic locative 'at the rear/behind the road' (پس + پشت intensifies 'behind' and راه = 'road/way'). Thus the clause conveys the content of the message.
segments.s309-g97: سپَه (subject) + بازگشتند (3rd person plural past) is a straightforward subject–verb clause: “the army returned/withdrew.” چُـون introduces a temporal subordinate clause here (classical use of چُن/چون = “when/as”), and تیره گشت is a past-clause (گشت = “became/turned”) meaning “it turned dark.” The whole segment reads: “The army withdrew when it became dark.”
segments.s309-g98: که here links the result/continuation clause to the previous clause (roughly “so that/and”), چشم سواران is a genitive construction “the riders’ eyes,” همی is the archaic/progressive particle often marking ongoing or immediate action before the verb, and خیره گشت is a past construction meaning “became fixed/stared.” The clause means “and the horsemen’s eyes were fixed/stared.”
segments.s309-g99: سپهدار is the head noun 'the commander.' با + a list of nouns (فر و نیرنگ و ساز) is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with glory and stratagem and music.' The nouns are coordinated by و; فر here is the epic sense 'royal glory,' نیرنگ 'stratagem/cunning,' ساز refers to music/instrument.
segments.s309-g100: چو آمد is a temporal clause head (چو = 'when/as' + آمد = 'he came'); به لشکرگه خویش is the prepositional phrase 'to/into his camp' (لشکرگه = 'army‑camp', خویش = 'his own' formed from خوی + suffix ش), and باز at the end is an adverb 'again/returned.' Word order (verb before some modifiers) and the postposed باز is typical of classical Persian: 'when he came back to his camp.'
segments.s309-g101: چنین گفت is a formulaic start 'thus said' (گفت here functions as the past verb 'said'). با طوس marks the addressee ('to/with Tous'—با used to indicate the person addressed), and کامروز ('today') locates the saying in time; جنگ follows as the noun 'battle/fight' that is being spoken about. Classical Persian often places the reporting verb early and then the content or addressee.
segments.s309-g102: نه is the negative particle 'not.' بر آرزو is a prepositional phrase here meaning roughly 'for (mere) desire/for a whim' (بر in classical usage can mark motive/reason), کرد is the past verb 'did/made' used here in the sense 'wage/do [battle],' and پور پشنگ — placed after the verb as is common in Persian — is the nominative/agent 'Pūr Pashang' (the son of Pashang) who is doing the speaking. The whole clause conveys: '[He says:] do not fight for desire' or, reading syntactically, 'Pūr Pashang did not fight for desire' as the intended admonition.
segments.s309-g103: گمانم is the speaker's verb/noun with 1st‑person enclitic 'I suppose/I think.' که introduces the subordinate clause ('that'), امشب is the adverb 'tonight,' and شبیخون کند is a verb phrase where کند (present/subjunctive form) expresses the action 'will make/do' — here 'will carry out a night‑raid.' In classical Persian کند often expresses prospective or habitual action.
segments.s309-g104: ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' دل‌درد دیرینه is the object phrase 'long‑standing heart‑ache/sorrow' (دیرینه = 'long‑standing/old'), بیرون (literally 'out') is an adverbial element, and کند repeats the verbal idea 'will make/drive out.' The construction 'ز ... بیرون کند' means 'drive out of/from ...'; both verbs in the subordinate clause (شبیخون کند and بیرون کند) are predicated by گمانم through که, with the same implied subject.
segments.s309-g113: Literal word order is 'to Tous spahbed gave that troop' — Persian allows the indirect object (به طوس سپهدار) before the verb. داد is the simple past 'gave'; آن گروه is the definite direct object (here without را, made definite by آن). سپهدار is an appositive/title attached to طوس: 'Tous, the spahbed.'
segments.s309-g114: بفرمود is the past-honorific/polite past of فرمودن meaning 'he ordered/commanded.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that'), and رفت here is a past-form used in this classical construction to express the commanded action ('that they go'); بر سوی is a double prepositional phrase giving direction 'toward/onto the mountain' (بر + سوی + کوه).
segments.s309-g115: تهمتن (a proper epithet/name) is the subject placed before the verb. سپه را is the definite object (را marks it), and به هامون کشید is a predicate where کشید (here 'moved/led') governs the motion into the destination هامون ('the plain'); the verb is past tense. The object را shows that 'the army' is what he moved.
segments.s309-g116: سپهبد is the subject and is followed by the directional phrase سوی کوه ('toward the mountain') plus the adverb بیرون ('outwards'); کشید is repeated past tense 'drew/moved' and the object (the army) is omitted here by ellipsis, understood from the previous clause. The clause thus means 'the spahbed drew them out toward the mountain.'
segments.s309-g117: بفرمود 'he ordered' + تا introduces the commanded/result clause. دور ('a distance; away') is adverbial, بیرون is an adverb 'out/away,' and شوند is the classical subjunctive/intransitive form ('that they be/go'), so دور بیرون شوند = 'that they withdraw/go out to a distance.'
segments.s309-g118: چپ و راست هر دو is a coordinated subject meaning 'both the left and the right (flanks).' به هامون شود(ند) uses به + place to indicate movement 'to the plain' and شوند (here) marks the same subjunctive/optative sense used earlier — the clause says both flanks go/move onto the plain. هر دو ('each two') functions as 'both.'
segments.s309-g119: طلایه (the vanguard/forefront) is the subject and مدارند is a present plural verb meaning roughly 'they keep/hold (their position)' or 'they move/perform their duty in front' (here habitual/presentive). The following و شمع و چراغ lists instruments/lighting ('and candles and lamps'), connecting the scene.
segments.s309-g120: یکی ... و یکی ... is a split/distribution construction: 'one (group) toward X and one (group) toward Y.' سوی is the directional preposition 'toward,' دشت is 'plain.' The second راغ here names the other direction/place; context shows a place-name reading (two detachments sent different ways), so each یکی indicates a different detachment's direction.
segments.s309-g105: This clause means 'he ordered a detachment to set out on the road.' یکی (literally 'one / a certain') functions as 'a (single) detachment / one party'; کنده is used in the sense 'detached (party)' (an older, poetic sense). فرمود is a past tense reporting/command verb 'he commanded' and کردن here acts as a light/auxiliary verb forming the verbal action with گزین/کنده (i.e. 'to cause/send to go'); به راه is the direction phrase 'onto the road.' Word order is literary: object/party (یکی کنده) comes before the verb (فرمود).
segments.s309-g106: بر آن‌سو is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward that side.' که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'where / in which.' بد is an epithet 'bad, wicked' modifying شاه 'king,' and توران specifies the domain 'Turan.' سپاه is the head noun 'army' — the phrase means 'that side where the wicked king of Turan has his army.' The sequence places modifiers before the final head noun in characteristic epic word order.
segments.s309-g107: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus,' گفت 'said'); the following کـ- is the cliticized که introducing the content clause. آتش 'the fire' is the subject of نسوزید, which is past tense with the negative prefix ن- meaning 'did not burn.' کس follows and, with the negative, carries the sense 'no one.' Word order is literary/inverted: the negative verb نسوزید precedes the indefinite/no-one کس.
segments.s309-g108: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it must not / one should not.' That (که) introduces the subordinate clause whose verb is آید (present-subjunctive/optative sense here: 'should come'). خروش جرس is a nominal phrase 'the cry/clamor (خروش) of the bell (جرس)' — together meaning 'the ringing/clamor of the bell.' So the whole clause says such ringing ought not to occur.
segments.s309-g109: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' لشکر سواران is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the army of horsemen.' که بودند is a relative clause 'who were,' and گرد here is an adverb/adverbial predicate meaning 'about/around' (i.e. 'who were around/nearby'). The clause as a whole identifies the source group 'from the horsemen who were about.'
segments.s309-g110: گزین کرد is a compound where گزین (a verbal noun/adjective 'selected') takes the light verb کرد to make the past action 'selected.' شاه is the agent, placed after the verb in poetic order: 'the king selected.' و = 'and'; به رستم سپرد is a simple transfer construction 'he entrusted (them) to Rostam,' with سپرد past 'entrusted/handed over' and به رستم marking the recipient.
segments.s309-g111: دگر modifies بهره to mean 'another share/contingent.' بگزید is the past-tense verb 'he chose/selected.' ز ایرانیان is the source phrase 'from the Iranians.' The word order puts the quantifier/adjective before the noun and the source preposition after the verb in a compact epic style.
segments.s309-g112: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'so that / who.' بندند is a present/jussive form from بستن used here in the sense 'be bound/committed' or 'be set' (i.e. 'will be committed to do X') rather than a literal tying. بر تاختن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'to charge/attack on(upon),' with بر marking the direction onto the action; بر میان = 'into the midst' (بر + میان). The clause therefore expresses purpose/intended action: they are to be committed to charging into the middle.
segments.s309-g33: This is a nominal sentence with no overt copula: 'تو' (vocative/pronoun) + predicate noun 'فرزندی' (a youth/offspring). The conjunction 'و' links the next predicate 'شاه ایران'. The final 'توی' here functions as a locative/standing particle (classical/poetic usage) meaning 'in' or 'among', preparing the locative sense carried into the next segment.
segments.s309-g34: The prepositional phrase 'به رزم اندرون' = 'into/within battle' (به + noun + اندرون). 'چنگ شیران' is a possessive/genitive image: 'the claws (چنگ) of the lions (شیران)'. The final 'توی' repeats the locative sense 'among/in', so the whole segment says you are 'in the clutch of lions' when in battle.
segments.s309-g35: 'یکی' here is an indefinite 'a certain' preceding the noun 'رزمگاهی' (battlefield). 'گزین' is an imperative verb 'choose' (archaic/poetic form). The postposed adjective 'دوردست' specifies the chosen battlefield as 'far‑off'.
segments.s309-g36: The negation 'نه' ('not') negates the following locative phrase. 'بر دامن' is a common prepositional expression 'upon the hem/lap of' (here figuratively 'under the protection of'); 'مرد خسروپرست' is a genitive phrase 'a man who worships Khosrow' (i.e. a sycophant).
segments.s309-g37: 'بگردیم' is a hortative/subjunctive verb form 'let us turn/go' (first‑person plural wish/suggestion). 'هر دو' ('each two' = 'both of us') is the subject phrase, and 'به آوردگاه' is the destination 'to the battlefield'. The verb precedes the subject here (typical of poetry/hortative construction).
segments.s309-g38: The second prepositional phrase 'به جایی' = 'to a place'. 'کز' is the relative particle 'from which', referring back to 'جایی' (so 'to a place from which...'). In the relative clause the verb 'ماند' (remain) appears before its subject 'سپاه' in inverted poetic order: 'دور ماند سپاه' = 'the army remained far (away)'.
segments.s309-g39: 'اگر' introduces a conditional clause. 'من' is the subject and 'شوم' is the subjunctive/hypothetical 'I should be' (precatory/conditional). 'کشته' is a past participle 'slain' functioning as complement of 'شوم'. The prepositional phrase 'بر دست تو' marks the agent/instrument 'by your hand' in older poetic usage.
segments.s309-g40: This clause reports the consequence: 'ز دریا' = 'from the sea' (poetic 'ز' = از). 'نهنگ آورد' = 'a whale will bring' (آورد as future/resultive in narration). The following 'شست' is the past participle of 'to wash' used here to describe the brought person as 'washed' (i.e., 'brought up washed'), and final 'تو' is the pronoun 'you' (object). The line uses condensed poetic word order: verb (آورد) + participle (شست) + pronoun (تو).
segments.s309-g9: همانا is an emphatic adverb meaning “truly” or “verily” and opens the clause. که here is the conjunction/relative connector introducing the statement that follows. فرسنگ (a unit of distance) appears before its numeral هزار for poetic inversion (normally هزار فرسنگ) and ز is the poetic form of از ‘from’, marking ایران as the point of departure.
segments.s309-g10: بود is the past existence verb “was/were” (here indicating there were). تا is used in the spatial sense “as far as.” به marks motion/toward and introduces the place reached, گنگ is here a toponym (not matching the glosses supplied) and is therefore marked as -1; اندر‌ای (اندر + vocative/locative particle -ای) means ‘in/at’ and شهریار is the head noun ‘sovereign/king’. Word order follows classical/poetic Persian where the verb (بود) appears before the directional phrase.
segments.s309-g11: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. The nouns are listed in pairs with و ‘and’ joining them (ریگ و بیابان = sand and desert). وز is a compacted poetic form (functioning like ‘and from’) not represented in the provided senses, so it is treated as -1 here; it links کوه (mountain) and the following شخ (a terse/onomatopoeic word for rocky crag). The sequence names multiple terrains as sources of the coming host.
segments.s309-g12: لشکر ‘army/host’ is the main noun; بر این‌سان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ‘in this manner’ (بر + demonstrative این‌سان) describing their appearance. چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’ and links مور و ملخ (‘ants and locusts’). The verb is implied from the previous clause (poetic ellipsis) so the phrase reads as a predicate describing how the two hosts appeared.
segments.s309-g13: زمین is the subject ‘the earth/land’. همچو دریا شد uses همچو + noun + شد ‘became like a sea’ (شاد as intransitive ‘to become’). از introduces the cause or material ‘from/with’ and links خون کین (‘the blood of vengeance’); here خون denotes bloodshed and کین qualifies it as vengeful hatred/vengeance.
segments.s309-g14: This is a secondary prepositional list giving the geographical extent. ز (from) + گنگ (here a place-name; not matching the glosses provided, so marked -1) and ز چین (‘from China’) set the span; تا functions as ‘as far as’ and به ایران‌زمین marks the endpoint ‘to the land of Iran’. Word order lists sources first then the limit with تا + به.
segments.s309-g15: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’. خون آن کشتگان را is the definite object (‘the blood of those slain’), with را as the object marker. ز خاک is a prepositional phrase ‘from the soil/earth’ specifying origin. Word order places the conditional opener and the object phrase before the rest of the clause, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s309-g16: به ژرفی is an adverbial phrase ‘into/to the depths’. برد here is the main past verb ‘to carry/take’ (the senses provided did not list the verbal meaning, so it is marked -1). رای یزدان پاک is a genitive phrase ‘the decree/counsel of the pure God’ (رای = divine decision/counsel, یزدان = God, پاک = pure), and the verb takes that object as its agent/author in this poetic construction.
segments.s309-g41: This is a negative imperative unit addressed to the addressee. تو (you) is vocative/second person (poetic). با خویش و پیوند مادر is a coordination: خویش = 'one's kin/relations' (here built from خوی + possessive ش referring back to 'تو'), and پیوند مادر = 'the mother's tie/bond'. مکوش is the negative imperative (مکن + suffix) meaning 'do not do' or 'do not sever'. Word order is fronted for emphasis (object/phrase before the verb).
segments.s309-g42: به پرهیز is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with caution/forbearance' (به functioning adverbially). وز here is poetic and means 'the weight/burden (of)' rather than 'wind'; کینه is 'hatred/grudge'. چندین modifies کینه as 'many a / several grudges'. مجوش is another negative imperative (مجو), 'do not seek / do not cherish'. The clause thus exhorts avoidance of the burden of many hatreds.
segments.s309-g43: وگر introduces a conditional ('if'). تو شوی combines the second-person singular form شوی (subjunctive/present of شدن) with the preceding pronoun to mean 'if you become / should be'. کشته is a past participle used predicatively ('slain'); بر دست من is an idiom 'by my hand' (i.e. 'at my hand' = 'by me'). The clause reads 'if you are slain by my hand.'
segments.s309-g44: به زنهار یزدان is an oath or appeal ('by the warning/admonition of God' or roughly 'for God's sake / I swear by God'); به here functions adverbially/introduces the formulaic oath. کز آن انجمن begins a relative/subordinate link (کز = 'that/which' or 'from which') referring forward to the assembly mentioned in context; انجمن = 'assembly/meeting.' Grammatically this is a sentence-medial oath/clause marker linking to what follows.
segments.s309-g45: نمانم is the first-person negative form of ماندن (here a volitive/performative: 'I will not remain' / 'I will not allow it to be that'). که introduces the subordinate clause. یک تن is an indefinite noun phrase 'one person' (تن = person). بپیچد is a third-person singular present/jussive form of پیچیدن 'to writhe/turn' (the prefix بـ is the classical present/jussive marker); together the clause means 'that one person may writhe from/with pain'. ز is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from' and درد is the noun 'pain'.
segments.s309-g46: دگر here functions adverbially 'thereafter/then/again'. بیند is third-person singular present/subjunctive of دیدن 'he sees' and continues the subordinate sequence begun by که. از is the preposition 'from/of', باد is the noun 'wind', خاک is 'dust/earth'. نبرد in this line functions as a verbal form meaning '(is) carried (away)' (classical Persian uses نـ + برد for negative/jussive shapes and older spellings), but the lemma senses provided do not include this verbal sense, so the dataset sense for نبرد does not match the usage here.
segments.s309-g47: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic). گوینده is the agent phrase 'the speaker'. بشنید is the simple past third-person form 'he heard'. خسرو (Khosrow) is the subject; Persian permits verb-before-subject word order so 'بشنید خسرو پیام' reads 'Khosrow heard the message'. پیام is the direct object 'message/tidings'.
segments.s309-g48: چنین 'thus/so' links to reportive speech. گفت is the past-tense verb 'he said' (here functioning as the speech verb introducing direct speech); the provided lemma senses list does not include this verbal sense. با plus a name is used here in the sense 'to'/'addressing' (classical usage allows با = to/with when used with گفت). پور functions as a patronymic marker 'son (of)'; دستان is the father's name (here part of the patronymic 'son of Dastan'); سام is the addressee's personal name. Together با پور دستان سام = 'to Sam, son of Dastan.'
segments.s309-g25: ز here introduces a cause ('because of, from'); کین is the noun 'hatred/vengeance' governed by ز. پدر‌گر follows as a modifier specifying whose hatred (literally 'fatherly' / 'of the father'). دل‌ + ت is a possessive construction: دل (heart) + second-person clitic -ت = 'your heart'. خیره is an adjectival/predicative element meaning 'stunned/astonished', and شد is the past copula 'became' (so خیره شد = 'became stunned'). Word order is causal preposition + noun phrase, then subject (دل‑ت) + predicate (خیره شد).
segments.s309-g26: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. آب here is used figuratively ('reputation, life, standing') and takes the following possessive: من after the noun marks the possessor ('my'); پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'before/in your presence'. تیره is an adjectival predicate 'dark/bleak' and شد is the past copula 'became' (تیره شد = 'became dark'). Note the noun + possessor order (آب من) and the simple past result clause.
segments.s309-g27: از آن بد is a causal/locative phrase: از ('because of, from') + آن بد ('that evil/that misfortune'). سیاوش is the subject and گنهکار is a predicate adjective 'guilty/sinful'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause literally reads 'Because of that evil, Siavash was guilty.'
segments.s309-g28: مرا (dative/experiencer 'to me' / 'for me') fronted here gives the perspective ('for me, ...'). دل is the subject 'heart'. پر + از expresses 'full of' (پر 'full', followed by از 'of'), so پر از درد و تیمار = 'full of pain and care/solicitude'. بود again is the past copula 'was'. The whole means 'my heart was full of pain and solicitude.'
segments.s309-g29: دگر here signals contrast/another mode ('another/different' or 'now, on the other hand'). گردش اختران بلند is a noun phrase: گردش 'turning, orbit' of اختران (stars) modified by بلند 'high/lofty' — Persian places the adjective after the noun. This segment supplies the subject/topic for the following relative clause.
segments.s309-g30: که is a relative/conjunction 'which/for' linking to گردش اختران بلند. هم ... و هم ... is the correlative 'both ... and ...'. با here means 'with/bringing' and پناهند (used as 'refuge/protection' or 'a shelter') and گزند 'harm' form the two coordinated nouns: 'both a shelter and a harm.' The clause says the stars' turning brings both protection and danger.
segments.s309-g31: مرا (for me/to me) marks the experiencer; سالیان شست here should be read as an expression of elapsed years: سالیان 'years' and شست (archaic شصت) = 'sixty'. بر سر گذشت is an idiom 'passed over the head' = 'elapsed'. گذشت is the past verb 'passed'. The clause means 'sixty years passed over me.'
segments.s309-g32: که introduces a dependent clause giving the accompanying fact: با نامداری = 'with/for renown' (نامداری = renown). نرفتم is the negative past first-person 'I did not go' (ن + رفتم). به دشت 'to the plain' (دشت often idiomatically means the battlefield). The clause = 'that I did not go to the plain for renown.'
segments.s309-g17: همانا is an emphatic adverb meaning 'truly/indeed'. چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause here ('when'); the phrase دریای قلزم is a construct (دریای + قلزم) meaning 'the Sea of Qalzam', and شود is the present/subjunctive form of شدن used to mark 'becomes / is (made)'. Word order is classical: 'چو + NP + شود' = 'when the Sea of Qalzam is (so)'.
segments.s309-g18: دو لشکر is the quantified subject 'two hosts/armies' (دو precedes لشکر). به خون اندرون is a locative phrase 'into/in blood' (به ... اندرون = 'within'), and گم شود is a passive/intransitive construction 'be lost / become lost' with شود as the verbal ending; overall: 'two armies will be lost in blood.'
segments.s309-g19: اگر ... خواهی is a standard conditional: 'if you want'. گنج is the direct object 'treasure'. ز marks source 'from (me)'; the sequence ز من‌گر سپاه is poetic/elliptical: the verb خواهی is understood to govern both گنج and the following noun سپاه (i.e. 'if you want treasure from me, [or if you want] an army'), and من‌گر is an archaic/contracted expression used in this poetic compression.
segments.s309-g20: وگر introduces an alternative conditional 'or if / but if'. بوم ترکان is a genitive/construct meaning 'the homeland of the Turks' (بوم + ترکان). و تخت و کلاه are coordinate nouns ('and throne and hat/crown') linked by و; the verb خواهی from the previous clause is omitted (ellipsis) and supplied by context.
segments.s309-g21: سپارم is first‑person singular (present/subjunctive or enjunctive) of سپاریدن 'I entrust / I hand over'. The clitic ت (here written separately) marks the second‑person object ('you'), and را is the definite object marker. The following clause من شوم ناپدید is independent: من is subject 'I', شوم is the first‑person subjunctive/optative of شدن ('may I become / I become'), and ناپدید is predicate 'vanished/invisible' — i.e. 'I will hand you over; I will vanish.'
segments.s309-g22: جز از introduces the exception 'except by / except from'. تیغ جان را is a possessive/partitive pattern where جان را (the life, with object marker را) combines with تیغ as the limiting phrase 'the key of life' or 'for life by the sword'. ندارم is 'I do not have' (present negative of داشتن) and کلید is the missing noun 'key'; the phrase order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: 'I have no key to life except the sword.'
segments.s309-g23: مکن‌گر is an archaic/poetic particle combining negation/conditional (roughly 'do not / if you would not'). The clitic ت (ترا = 'you') plus را marks the 2nd‑person object. The nominal sequence من پدر مادرم followed by the copula (next token) expresses an identity/claim; word order is poetic/inverted (subject and kinship term cluster together before the copula), yielding something like 'I am your maternal grandfather' or 'I am the father of your mother' depending on context.
segments.s309-g24: ز تخم فریدون is a genitive phrase 'of the seed/stock of Fereydun' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic usage, تخم = 'seed/lineage', فریدون a proper name). افسونگرم is a noun plus the first‑person copular suffix -م ('I am an enchanter/sorcerer'), so the clause states origin and profession: 'of the seed of Fereydun, I am an enchanter.'
segments.s309-g57: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him' placed before the verb. گفت is the past tense verb 'said'; in poetic word order the subject رستم follows the verb. که‌ای is an archaic vocative ('که ای' = 'O ...') used to call someone, here followed by شهریار 'sovereign / king'.
segments.s309-g58: بدین functions as 'in/with this' (classical contraction of به + این) introducing location; در مدار = 'in the circle / within the circuit' (مدار = circuit, ring); آتش 'fire' names what is in the circle; اندر کنار = 'at the side / beside' (اندر = in/within, کنار = edge/beside). The whole phrase describes location: 'in this circle beside the fire.'
segments.s309-g59: که introduces the clause 'that / for'; ننگست is an impersonal or predicative form meaning 'it is shameful' (classical Persian adjective-like predicate). بر شاه رفتن uses the infinitive رفتن with بر marking the person ('for/against the king' here meaning 'for a king to go'); به جنگ 'to war' completes the idea 'to go to war.'
segments.s309-g60: وگر = 'and if / but if' sets a conditional. همنبرد تو = 'your fellow-combatant' (noun همنبرد with the possessor suffix تو placed after the noun, as is common in classical Persian). باشد is a copular auxiliary ('is' / subjunctive‑style 'be') linking to the name پشنگ, here identifying the fellow-combatant as Pashang.
segments.s309-g61: دگر 'another / other' introduces a different person; آن‌که = 'the one who' (relative); گوید is the literary present 'says' (used in narrative/direct speech); که introduces the reported speech; با لشکرم = 'with my army' (لشکر 'army' + possessive suffix -م 'my').
segments.s309-g62: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (used before a verbal noun or verb); جنگ here is the object 'war/fighting' ('do not make war'); با دوده و کشورم links two objects with با 'with' — دوده is ambiguous in meaning (in the given lexicon 'smoke/soot', but in context it may be a name or a term), and کشورم = 'my country/realm' (کشور + -م).
segments.s309-g63: ز ... به ... is the classical poetic 'from ... to ...' construction (ز دریا به دریا = 'from sea to sea'). ترا here is the oblique form of 'تو' (split in the text as ت + را) marking 'to/for you' or 'your' in a possessive sense; لشکرست is a nominal predicate 'is an army / is a host' (لشکر + -ست copula). The line says your forces extend from one sea to the other.
segments.s309-g64: کجا is a rhetorical 'where / how' asking for comparison; رایشان (here 'رأی‌شان') means 'their counsel / opinion' (the lexicon entry shown corresponds to را but the reading here is رأی‌شان 'their counsel'); ز این سخن = 'from / to this speech' (ز = 'from/with regard to'); دیگرست is 'is other / differs' (predicate meaning 'is different'). The clause rhetorically asks how their counsel could differ from what has been said.

sentences.s309-s61: Know this, that if Afrasiab makes a night-raid against him while he is asleep.
sentences.s309-s62: If an army should come — and another comes up from the rear — he will remain without any rescuer.
sentences.s309-s37: He professed even greater fidelity to Siavash than that; his speech was full of enchantment, his heart full of treachery.
sentences.s309-s38: A spahbed does not gain glory by crookedness; his tongue was bewildering and full of radiance, and his heart full of lies.
sentences.s309-s39: If anyone appears whose counsel is warlike and sufficient, there is no one to fight him except me.
sentences.s309-s40: Tahmāten is in his place and Giv is brave; they seek battle with elephants and lions.
sentences.s309-s41: If a king seeks battle with a king, why should this plain be filled with men?
sentences.s309-s42: There will be no more war between me and you than this; you will now see a dark and narrow day.
sentences.s309-s43: The messenger returned and came like the wind; having heard everything, he put the news before him (reminded him).
sentences.s309-s44: Afrasiab’s soul became full of sorrow; he did not at all hasten to go to war.
sentences.s309-s25: That this treacherous Turk who deceives the people will never behold a descent from on high.
sentences.s309-s26: He sought from our hands such a stratagem, that he might sit upon the throne of Iran.
sentences.s309-s27: From many reports he would continually say, unless he seeks the tomb of the love‑struck.
sentences.s309-s28: If he brings with him a scion of Fereydun and the son Pashang, it would not be a disgrace to me.
sentences.s309-s45: The king brought the host into battle; the other army was forced to move.
sentences.s309-s46: Some (came) with delay and some with haste; the earth became like a sea of water.
sentences.s309-s47: As if arrows rained from the clouds — drops (like dew) kept falling upon him and his tiger.
sentences.s309-s48: From nightfall until the sun turned ruby, the earth was full of blood beneath the horseshoes.
sentences.s309-s33: If you make a covenant with God and the forefathers, it is not fitting that the elixir should be in the heart.
sentences.s309-s34: Lead the dense host into battle; (but) some words were tainted and wicked.
sentences.s309-s35: When Khosrow heard Rostam's words, he conceived another thought.
sentences.s309-s36: He said to the speaker: "This ill‑willed man clung to me like this in the battle."
sentences.s309-s1: After that a cry rose from the host, and earth and time became full of war and tumult.
sentences.s309-s2: A star appeared from a dark whirl of dust; the yellow face of the sun became lapis-lazuli.
sentences.s309-s3: The commander of the Turks, from that assembly, chose two experienced men.
sentences.s309-s4: He sent a message to the king, saying that many Kurds were behind the road.
sentences.s309-s49: The army withdrew when it grew dark, so that the horsemen's eyes stared in astonishment.
sentences.s309-s50: The commander, with glory, stratagem, and music, returned to his own camp.
sentences.s309-s51: Thus Pūr Pashang said to Tous: 'Today, do not fight for mere desire.'
sentences.s309-s52: I think that tonight he will make a night-raid and drive out a long-standing sorrow from his heart.
sentences.s309-s57: He gave that troop to Tous, the spahbed, and ordered them to go toward the mountain.
sentences.s309-s58: Tahmētan led the army onto the plain; the spahbed drew them out toward the mountain.
sentences.s309-s59: He ordered that they withdraw some distance; both left and right went onto the plain.
sentences.s309-s60: The vanguard keep watch, and with candles and lamps one party toward the plain and the other toward Ragh.
sentences.s309-s53: He ordered a detachment to set out on the road toward that side where the wicked king of Turan had his army.
sentences.s309-s54: He said thus: since that fire burned no one, there should be no ringing of the bell.
sentences.s309-s55: From the army of horsemen who were about, the king selected [men] and entrusted them to Rostam.
sentences.s309-s56: He chose another contingent from the Iranians, so that they should be set to charge into the midst.
sentences.s309-s17: You are a youth and the king of Iran; in battle you are among the claws of lions.
sentences.s309-s18: Choose a far‑off battlefield; not upon the lap (i.e. under the protection) of a king‑worshipping man.
sentences.s309-s19: Let the two of us go to the battlefield — to a place from which the army stays far away.
sentences.s309-s20: If I should be slain by your hand, a whale will bring you up washed from the sea.
sentences.s309-s5: Truly, there were a thousand parasangs from Iran as far as the Ganges, to the sovereign.
sentences.s309-s6: From the sands and wilderness, and from the mountains and crags, two hosts came — in this manner like ants and locusts.
sentences.s309-s7: The earth became like a sea from the blood of vengeance, from the Ganges and from China as far as the land of Iran.
sentences.s309-s8: If the blood of those slain were taken from the soil into the depths by the pure God's decree.
sentences.s309-s21: Do not break with your kin and your mother's bond; be cautious — do not harbor many grudges.
sentences.s309-s22: And if you should be slain by my hand — by God's warning, from that assembly...
sentences.s309-s23: I will not remain so that one person writhes in pain and then sees his dust carried off by the wind.
sentences.s309-s24: The king heard the message from the speaker; thus he said to Sam, son of Dastan.
sentences.s309-s13: Because of my father's hatred your heart was stunned; thus my standing before you was darkened.
sentences.s309-s14: Because of that ill/evil, Siavash was guilty; my heart was full of pain and solicitude.
sentences.s309-s15: The turning of the lofty stars is different, for it is both a refuge and a source of harm.
sentences.s309-s16: Sixty years passed over me; I did not go to the plain for renown.
sentences.s309-s9: Truly, when the Sea of Qalzam is reached, two hosts will be swallowed up in blood.
sentences.s309-s10: If you want treasure from me — or an army; or the Turks' homeland, and throne and crown.
sentences.s309-s11: I will hand you over and vanish; I have no key to life except the sword.
sentences.s309-s12: Do not (do so) — I am your maternal grandfather; of the seed of Fereydun I am an enchanter.
sentences.s309-s29: Rostam said to him, "O sovereign, do not seat yourself in this circle beside the fire."
sentences.s309-s30: For it is a shame for a king to go to war; but if your fellow-combatant is Pashang...
sentences.s309-s31: As for another — the one who says — 'Do not make war with my army; do not fight with Dodeh and my country.'
sentences.s309-s32: From sea to sea your army stretches; where would their counsel be other than this speech?
